 {"id":"bd-19qi","title":"demo_showcase features: pixel rendering showpiece (PixelBuffer + Grayscale + supersampling)","description":"# demo_showcase Features — Pixel Rendering Showpiece (PixelBuffer + Grayscale + Supersampling)

## Purpose

Pixel-level rendering is a unique capability for a terminal UI engine. This bead ensures the demo has an unmistakable “graphics” moment using:

- `PixelBuffer` (RGBA pixels)
- `GrayscaleBuffer` (intensity buffers)
- `OptimizedBuffer::draw_supersample_buffer` (2x2 quadrants)
- `draw_grayscale_buffer_unicode` for sparklines / heatmaps

## Requirements

- The preview panel must render:
  - an animated effect (gradient waves, plasma, noise, or oscilloscope)
  - a small chart (sparkline) driven by deterministic metrics

- The render should be clipped to the panel via scissor.
- Add a semi-transparent UI overlay (labels + toggles) on top of the pixels to demonstrate blending.

## Acceptance Notes

- The preview looks visually impressive (not just static blocks).
- The effect is smooth under the frame pacing system.
- Works in reduced-color terminals with a graceful palette fallback.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- The preview panel renders a clearly animated pixel effect using `PixelBuffer` + supersampling (not just static blocks).
- The panel also renders at least one grayscale-driven visualization (sparkline/heatmap) using `GrayscaleBuffer` helpers.
- Pixel drawing is clipped to the panel’s inner rect via scissor (no bleed).
- A translucent UI overlay (labels/toggles) is drawn over the pixels to visibly prove alpha blending.
- With reduced-color constraints (e.g., `--cap-preset no_truecolor` in headless), the preview still looks good via a graceful palette fallback.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:28:17.567728370Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T03:35:19.700406108Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T03:35:19.700337090Z","close_reason":"Pixel rendering showpiece implemented: animated gradient orb (PixelBuffer with HSV color rotation), FPS sparkline (GrayscaleBuffer with Unicode shade), alpha blending glass overlay.  {"id":"bd-1al","title":"demo_showcase UI: overlay system (modals + glass + focus capture)","description":"# demo_showcase UI — Overlay System (Modals + Glass + Focus Capture)

## Purpose

The demo app needs multiple overlays that all share the same core behavior:

- semi-transparent “glass” panels layered over the main UI
- dimmed / tinted backdrop
- animated enter/exit (subtle fade / slide)
- input capture (overlay takes focus; underlying UI stops receiving events)
- hit testing for clickable controls inside overlays

This bead builds a reusable overlay foundation so **Help**, **Command Palette**, and **Tour** don’t each reinvent modal behavior.

## Core Requirements

### Rendering

- Overlays render in the final render pass (highest z-order).
- Backdrop uses `push_opacity()` + translucent `fill_rect()` so alpha blending is obvious.
- Overlay body uses:
  - rounded/double border styles
  - soft gradients / subtle noise patterns (procedural) to look “premium”
  - clipped internal scrolling content (scissor stack)

### Input / Focus

- Overlay manager owns:
  - `active: Option<Overlay>` (or a small stack if needed)
  - `focus: OverlayFocus` (which control is focused)
- When an overlay is active:
  - keyboard shortcuts are handled by overlay first
  - mouse hit testing only targets overlay regions
  - optional “click outside to dismiss” behavior

### HitGrid integration

- Overlay rendering must also register hit areas (`Renderer::register_hit_area`).
- Use `Renderer::push_hit_scissor` around overlay internals so hit areas respect clipping.

## Suggested Structure (demo-only)

Inside `src/bin/demo_showcase.rs`:

- `enum Overlay { Help(HelpState), Palette(PaletteState), Tour(TourState) }`
- `struct OverlayManager { active: Option<Overlay>, anim: OverlayAnim, ... }`
- `fn render_overlays(&mut self, ctx: &mut RenderCtx, ...)` called from the render pass bead.

## Acceptance Notes

We consider this “done” when:

- The overlay visuals clearly demonstrate alpha blending + opacity stack (no “flat” look).
- Focus is trapped inside overlays and underlying UI does not react.
- Overlay hit targets work and do not leak outside clipped regions.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Overlays render above the base UI with an obvious dimmed backdrop and glassy modal body using alpha blending.
- Overlays have subtle enter/exit animation (fade/slide) driven by the animation clock.
- When an overlay is active, input is captured: underlying UI does not react.
- Overlay hit testing works for internal controls and respects hit scissoring for clipped regions.
- Help, Command Palette, and Tour reuse the same overlay foundation rather than reimplementing modal behavior.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:23:19.127313380Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:10:23.047477805Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T02:10:23.047407775Z","close_reason":"Implemented overlay system with OverlayManager, HelpState, PaletteState, TourState. Added draw_pass_overlays with backdrop dimming, glass panels, animated transitions. All three overlay types (Help, Palette, Tour) now render with proper alpha blending and decorative borders.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","ui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-1pd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-35g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-3l0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-c75","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1al","depends_on_id":"bd-po1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1bdn","title":"EPIC: Memory Safety - Integer Overflow Prevention","description":"## Overview

This epic addresses **CRITICAL MEMORY SAFETY BUGS** discovered during the deep codebase audit. Multiple locations in the buffer and rendering system use unchecked integer arithmetic that can overflow, leading to:

1. **Silent memory corruption** - Wrong indices access wrong data
2. **Out-of-bounds access** - Potential crashes or security issues
3. **Phantom references** - Pool IDs wrap around causing use-after-free scenarios

## Affected Systems

### Buffer Indexing (CRITICAL)
- src/buffer/mod.rs lines 143, 152, 639, 640, 712, 713
- src/buffer/pixel.rs lines 92, 101, 138, 147
- src/renderer/hitgrid.rs line 48

The pattern `y * width + x` is used throughout without overflow checking. For a 10,000x10,000 buffer, overflow occurs at y > 429,496.

### GraphemeId Width Encoding (CRITICAL)
- src/cell.rs lines 68-71
- src/grapheme_pool.rs lines 141-142, 156

Width values >127 are silently truncated because only 7 bits are allocated in the GraphemeId encoding. Width 128 becomes 0, causing incorrect rendering.

### Pool ID Overflow (CRITICAL)  
- src/grapheme_pool.rs line 150-151

The assert() that checks pool ID overflow is REMOVED in release builds. IDs can wrap around the 24-bit boundary, causing pool slot collisions and use-after-free bugs.

## Fix Strategy

1. Use checked/saturating arithmetic for all buffer index calculations
2. Add explicit bounds validation in GraphemeId::new()
3. Replace assert!() with explicit validation that works in release mode
4. Add overflow tests for each affected function

## Impact

Without these fixes, the library has undefined behavior with:
- Large terminal sizes
- Long-running sessions with many unique graphemes
- Complex emoji/grapheme content","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T23:02:28.674325205Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T23:16:47.460257043Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T23:16:47.460195358Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-1bo1","title":"Infrastructure: Test Coverage Reporting Setup","description":"# Test Coverage & Regression Detection Setup (Consolidated)

Consolidated from bd-1bo1 and bd-2cuu.

## Current State
- NO coverage tooling configured
- No coverage thresholds enforced
- No CI coverage reporting
- No performance regression detection

## Goals
1. Generate and track test coverage
2. Detect performance regressions
3. Fail CI on coverage drops
4. Archive test artifacts

## Tool Selection

**Recommendation: cargo-llvm-cov** for accuracy and cross-platform support.

Alternatives considered:
- cargo-tarpaulin (easiest, Linux-focused)
- grcov (Mozilla's tool)

## Coverage Configuration

### .cargo/config.toml
```toml
[llvm-cov]
exclude-patterns = ["tests/*", "benches/*"]
```

### Coverage Thresholds

| Module | Target | Rationale |
|--------|--------|-----------|
| Overall | ≥ 80% | Good baseline |
| src/color.rs | ≥ 95% | Core algorithm, must be correct |
| src/buffer/ | ≥ 90% | Critical path |
| src/renderer/ | ≥ 85% | Complex but testable |
| src/text/ | ≥ 85% | Well-tested already |
| src/terminal/ | ≥ 75% | Some FFI limits |
| src/input/ | ≥ 90% | Well-tested already |
| New code | 100% | All new code must be tested |

## CI Workflow

### .github/workflows/coverage.yml
```yaml
name: Coverage
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  coverage:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Install coverage tools
        run: cargo install cargo-llvm-cov
        
      - name: Run tests with coverage
        run: cargo llvm-cov --all-features --workspace --lcov --output-path lcov.info
        
      - name: Check thresholds
        run: cargo llvm-cov --fail-under-lines 80
        
      - name: Generate HTML report
        run: cargo llvm-cov --html --output-dir coverage
        
      - name: Upload to Codecov
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3
        with:
          files: lcov.info
          
      - name: Upload HTML report
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: coverage-report
          path: coverage/
```

## Performance Regression Detection

### Benchmark Tracking
```yaml
- name: Run benchmarks
  run: cargo bench --bench buffer -- --save-baseline ci

- name: Compare to main
  run: cargo bench --bench buffer -- --baseline main
  continue-on-error: true
```

### Metrics to Track
- Buffer operations (cells/sec)
- Diff computation (time)
- ANSI generation (bytes/sec)
- Text insertion (ops/sec)
- Alpha blending (ops/sec)

## Test Artifacts

### On Failure Archive
```yaml
- uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
  if: failure()
  with:
    name: test-artifacts
    path: |
      target/test-artifacts/
      target/llvm-cov/
```

### Artifact Contents
- JSONL logs from E2E tests
- Buffer dumps
- ANSI captures
- Stack traces
- Coverage HTML report

## Local Development

```bash
# Generate HTML coverage report
cargo llvm-cov --html

# View immediately
cargo llvm-cov --open

# Check thresholds locally
cargo llvm-cov --fail-under-lines 80
```

## Files to Create
- .github/workflows/coverage.yml
- codecov.yml (Codecov configuration)
- COVERAGE.md - workflow documentation

## Acceptance Criteria
- Coverage report generated in CI
- Thresholds enforced (fail on drop)
- Badge in README
- Local workflow documented
- Performance baseline established
- Artifacts captured on failure","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T18:46:48.952771258Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T01:25:57.722123914Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T01:25:57.722040799Z","close_reason":"Completed coverage infrastructure setup: created codecov.yml config, COVERAGE.md documentation, and added coverage badge to README. Coverage workflow already existed in ci.yml with module-specific thresholds.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1bo1","depends_on_id":"bd-1rfq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1bo1","depends_on_id":"bd-2e6f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1bo1","depends_on_id":"bd-h3gn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1by","title":"demo_showcase runtime: robust cleanup + panic hook (restore terminal on crash)","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Robust Cleanup + Panic Hook

## Purpose

Even though `Renderer` and `Terminal` try to restore state on Drop, a panic can still leave the user’s terminal in a bad state (especially if aborting).

This bead adds belt-and-suspenders cleanup so the demo is “safe to run”.

## Approach

- Install a panic hook that:
  - attempts best-effort cleanup
  - prints a helpful message (`reset` / `stty sane`)

Important:

- Keep it minimal and avoid introducing dependencies.
- Do not swallow panic info; chain to the original hook.

## Scope

This is demo-only code in `src/bin/demo_showcase.rs`.","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:
- [ ] Panic hook attempts terminal cleanup
- [ ] Original panic hook is preserved
- [ ] Crash instructions are printed clearly","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:11:17.714696250Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T04:37:16.145349704Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T04:37:16.145257883Z","close_reason":"Implemented panic hook with terminal cleanup: ANSI reset sequences, termios restoration, and recovery instructions","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","resilience","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1by","depends_on_id":"bd-29z","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1by","depends_on_id":"bd-z1b","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:38Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1n67","title":"demo_showcase tests: headless smoke run (cargo run -- --headless-smoke)","description":"# demo_showcase Tests — Headless Smoke Run

## Purpose

Compile coverage is not enough: we want a minimal runtime check that the demo can:

- initialize state
- execute update + render
- exercise diff computation

…without a real terminal.

## Implementation

Add a test that runs the binary in headless mode:

- cargo run --all-features --bin demo_showcase -- --headless-smoke --max-frames 10

Optionally force a fixed size for stability:

- --headless-size 80x24

## Output / Assertions

- Assert process exits 0.
- Assert stdout contains the stable success marker:
  - HEADLESS_SMOKE_OK

Optional (recommended for deeper regression coverage):

- run with --headless-dump-json and parse the JSON
- assert expected “sentinel markers” exist (top bar title, panel headers, etc.)

## Logging

- On failure, print the full stderr and stdout to aid debugging.
- If HARNESS_ARTIFACTS=1 is set, store stdout/stderr under target/test-artifacts.

## Acceptance Notes

- cargo test runs this and it works in CI (no TTY).
- Failures surface actionable logs.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- An integration test runs `demo_showcase` with `--headless-smoke` (and a bounded frame count) in a non-TTY environment.
- The test asserts: exit code 0 and stdout contains `HEADLESS_SMOKE_OK`.
- On failure, the test prints full stdout/stderr and includes a reproduction command.
- When `HARNESS_ARTIFACTS=1`, stdout/stderr (and any JSON dump) are written under `target/test-artifacts`.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:29:21.419793344Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T03:33:41.543592691Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T03:33:41.543524924Z","close_reason":"Headless smoke test implemented: validates full render pipeline with exit 0 and HEADLESS_SMOKE_OK marker assertion","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1n67","depends_on_id":"bd-10de","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:39Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1n67","depends_on_id":"bd-2el","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:39Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1n67","depends_on_id":"bd-383","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:39Z","created_by":"import"}]}

-{"id":"bd-1nfd","title":"Implement mark_region_dirty(rect) - Dirty region tracking","description":"# Task: Implement mark_region_dirty(rect)

## Function Signature (Rust)
```rust
pub fn mark_region_dirty(&mut self, rect: Rect)
```

## Purpose
Marks a rectangular region as needing re-render. Enables efficient partial updates when only part of the screen changes.

## Rect Type
```rust
pub struct Rect {
    pub x: u32,
    pub y: u32,
    pub width: u32,
    pub height: u32,
}
```

## Implementation Notes
1. Store dirty regions in a list or use dirty bitmap
2. Coalesce overlapping regions for efficiency
3. Used by diff renderer to skip unchanged areas
4. Clear dirty regions after present()

## Design Choices
- List of rects: simpler, good for sparse updates
- Bitmap: better for many small updates
- Quadtree: optimal for complex patterns (overkill?)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dirty region storage implemented
- [ ] Region coalescing (optional optimization)
- [ ] Integration with diff renderer
- [ ] Regions cleared after present
- [ ] Bounds checking

## Testing
- Single dirty region
- Multiple overlapping regions
- Region outside buffer bounds
- Clear after present","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-30T15:26:39.150532397Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-30T15:26:39.157348Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1nfd","depends_on_id":"bd-20k1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:39Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1nfp","title":"E2E: Input Simulation for Complex Workflows","description":"# E2E: Input Simulation & Event Flow Testing (Consolidated)

Consolidated from bd-1nfp and bd-3nbv.

## Scope
Test complete input handling from raw bytes → parsed events → application response, with comprehensive simulation utilities for complex workflows.

## Test Infrastructure

### InputSequence Builder
```rust
pub struct InputSequence {
    events: Vec<InputEvent>,
    timing: TimingMode, // Instant, Realistic, Stress
}

impl InputSequence {
    pub fn type_text(s: &str) -> Self;
    pub fn keystroke(key: KeyCode, mods: Modifiers) -> Self;
    pub fn mouse_click(x: u16, y: u16, button: MouseButton) -> Self;
    pub fn mouse_drag(from: (u16,u16), to: (u16,u16)) -> Self;
    pub fn paste(content: &str) -> Self;
    pub fn with_delay(self, ms: u64) -> Self;
}
```

### Timing Modes
- **Instant**: All events at once (stress testing)
- **Realistic**: 50-200 WPM typing speed simulation
- **Stress**: Rapid burst input

## Test Scenarios

### Keyboard Input Flow (6 tests)
- [ ] Single keypress → KeyEvent
- [ ] Modifier combinations (Ctrl+X, Alt+Y, Ctrl+Alt+Z)
- [ ] Function keys (F1-F12)
- [ ] Special keys (arrows, home, end, pgup, pgdn, insert, delete)
- [ ] Unicode character input (multi-byte UTF-8)
- [ ] Escape key handling (vs Alt prefix)

### Mouse Input Flow (5 tests)
- [ ] Click → MouseEvent with correct position
- [ ] Drag sequence (press → move → release)
- [ ] Scroll wheel events (up/down)
- [ ] Mouse position accuracy at boundaries
- [ ] Mouse button combinations (left+right)

### Paste Input (4 tests)
- [ ] Bracketed paste detection (\x1b[200~ ... \x1b[201~)
- [ ] Large paste handling (>10KB)
- [ ] Binary data in paste (no corruption)
- [ ] Paste with special characters and Unicode

### Event to Render Response (4 tests)
- [ ] Keypress → buffer change → present
- [ ] Mouse click → hit test → response render
- [ ] Resize event → buffer resize → present
- [ ] Focus change → application state update

### Complex Workflows (6 tests)
- [ ] Open file → edit → save simulation
- [ ] Navigate menu → select option → confirm
- [ ] Scroll → click → drag selection
- [ ] Search → navigate results → select
- [ ] Undo → redo → undo sequence
- [ ] Multi-window focus switching

### Rapid Input (3 tests)
- [ ] Typing speed simulation (50-200 WPM)
- [ ] Burst input (100+ keys/second)
- [ ] Input during rendering (race condition check)

### Edge Cases (4 tests)
- [ ] Partial escape sequences (interrupted input)
- [ ] Invalid/malformed sequences (graceful handling)
- [ ] Timeout handling (escape vs alt)
- [ ] Input queue overflow behavior


- Verify buffer state after each sequence

- No dropped events under stress

- tests/e2e_workflows.rs - complex workflow tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- 30+ input/workflow test scenarios
- Rapid input stress tests pass
- Mouse drag operations tested
- Paste edge cases covered
- All keyboard/mouse flows verified
- No dropped events under stress

 {"id":"bd-1nv2","title":"Fix resize parsing fallback to 24x80","description":"## Problem

src/input/parser.rs lines 395-406 falls back to 24x80 on parse failure:

```rust
if parts.len() >= 3 && parts[0] == "8" {
    let height: u16 = parts[1].parse().unwrap_or(24);  // WRONG DEFAULT
    let width: u16 = parts[2].parse().unwrap_or(80);   // WRONG DEFAULT
    return Ok((Event::Resize { width, height }, end + 1));
}
```

## Why This Is Wrong

Modern terminals are rarely 24x80. Using this as fallback:
- Makes application think terminal is tiny
- Causes incorrect layout calculations
- User sees broken UI
- No indication of the parse error

## Required Fix

Option A: Return parse error instead of fallback
```rust
let height: u16 = parts[1].parse()
    .map_err(|_| ParseError::InvalidResizeEvent)?;
let width: u16 = parts[2].parse()
    .map_err(|_| ParseError::InvalidResizeEvent)?;
```

Option B: Keep current size if parse fails
```rust
// Requires access to current size - may not be available in parser
```

**Recommendation:** Option A - fail explicitly rather than silently wrong.

## Additional Issues

The code only handles XTWINOPS format (CSI 8 ; height ; width t). Other formats:
- CSI 4 ; height ; width t (pixel size) - not handled
- Should document which resize formats are supported

## Files to Modify

- src/input/parser.rs: parse_csi() resize handling
- src/input/events.rs: Add ParseError::InvalidResizeEvent if needed

## Testing

1. Test valid resize sequences
2. Test malformed resize sequences (non-numeric)
3. Verify error is returned, not 24x80 fallback","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-28T23:06:15.534501904Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T23:56:29.995174591Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T23:56:29.995111202Z","close_reason":"Added ParseError::InvalidResizeFormat.  {"id":"bd-1nw7","title":"Implement compact() - Pool compaction","description":"# Task: Implement compact()

## Function Signature (Rust)
```rust
pub fn compact(&mut self) -> CompactionResult
```

## CompactionResult Type
```rust
pub struct CompactionResult {
    pub old_to_new: HashMap<u32, u32>,  // ID remapping
    pub slots_freed: usize,
    pub bytes_saved: usize,
}
```

## Purpose
Defragments the pool by removing gaps (None slots) and compacting active entries. This is complex because existing references must be updated.

## Implementation Complexity: HIGH
1. Build map of old_id -> new_id for active entries
2. Create new compact storage
3. Move entries to new positions
4. Return remapping for callers to update their references

## CRITICAL CONSIDERATION
All buffers holding grapheme IDs must update their references!
This requires coordination with Buffer module.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Removes freed slots
- [ ] Returns valid remapping
- [ ] Existing IDs remain valid (via remapping)
- [ ] Memory actually freed

## Testing
- Compact fragmented pool
- Verify remapping correctness
- Memory reduction verified","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-30T15:35:53.357388582Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-30T22:22:34.271725003Z","closed_at":"2026-01-30T22:22:34.271661815Z","close_reason":"Implemented compact() method with CompactionResult type and 14 comprehensive tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1nw7","depends_on_id":"bd-g3az","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-01T04:32:39Z","created_by":"import"}]}
